questions.question: 1. What is the fundamental difference between a "Mean" in K-Means and a "Medoid" in K-Medoids?
options: A) A mean is always an integer, while a medoid can be a decimal.
B) A mean is a theoretical average, while a medoid must be an actual data point from the dataset.
C) A mean ignores outliers, while a medoid is heavily influenced by them.
D) A mean is used for categorical data, while a medoid is used for numerical data.

questions.question: 2. Why are K-Medoids considered more "robust" than K-Means?
options: A) It runs much faster on large datasets.
B) It automatically determines the optimal number of clusters ($k$).
C) Its cluster centers are not easily pulled away by extreme outlier values.
D) It can calculate mathematical averages for text-based data.

questions.question: 3. In the K-Medoids (PAM) algorithm, what happens during the "Swap" phase?
options: A) Two different clusters swap their medoids with each other.
B) The algorithm temporarily swaps a medoid with a non-medoid point to see if the total error cost decreases.
C) The user swaps the distance metric from Euclidean to Manhattan.
D) Categorical data is swapped out for numerical data.

questions.question: 4. K-Medoids can cluster items like text documents or images because it can use a pre-calculated "Distance Matrix." Why does this give it an advantage over K-Means?
options: A) It allows K-Medoids to skip the initialization step.
B) K-Medoids only need to know the difference (distance) between two items, rather than needing to calculate a mathematical average.
C) A distance matrix automatically removes all outliers before the algorithm runs.
D) It converts all text documents into numerical averages.

questions.question: 5. How do you evaluate and choose the best number of clusters ($k$) for K-Medoids?
options: A) By counting the total number of data points and dividing by 10.
B) By running the algorithm once and letting it self-adjust.
C) By testing different $k$ values and choosing the one with the highest Silhouette Score.
D) By testing different $k$ values and choosing the one with the lowest Gamma weight.

questions.question: 6. What is the primary limitation of the standard K-Medoids (PAM) algorithm?
options: A) It is incredibly slow on large datasets because it tests so many potential swaps.
B) It cannot handle data that contains outliers.
C) It forces you to use Euclidean distance.
D) It only works on pure categorical data.

questions.question: 7. How does the CLARA algorithm solve the speed problem of standard K-Medoids?
options: A) It uses a supercomputer to calculate all swaps simultaneously.
B) It takes a small random sample of the data, finds the medoids for that sample, and applies them to the whole dataset.
C) It permanently deletes any data points that are too far away from the center.
D) It converts all medoids into theoretical means.

questions.question: 8. What is the key difference in how CLARANS searches for cluster centers compared to CLARA?
options: A) CLARANS searches purely numerical data, while CLARA searches text data.
B) CLARANS searches within one small sample, while CLARA searches the whole dataset.
C) CLARANS conducts a randomized search across the entire dataset by checking a limited number of "neighbor" swaps, rather than restricting itself to a single small sample.
D) CLARANS is slower than K-Medoids, but much more accurate.

questions.question: 9. Which of the following statements best describes K-Median?
options: A) Like K-Medoids, it uses an actual existing data point as its center.
B) It is highly sensitive to outliers.
C) It calculates a theoretical center using the middle value of the data, meaning the center point might not actually exist in the dataset.
D) It is an algorithm designed exclusively for categorical data.

questions.question: 10. You need to cluster 5 million customer records based on numerical spending habits. You want to avoid outlier interference, but standard K-Medoids will take too long. Which algorithm is best suited for a fast result?
options: A) K-Means
B) CLARA
C) K-Modes
D) K-Prototypes
